title: From Policy PDF to Enforced Rule
context: An architecture review is underway for how Expenses should move from uploaded policy documents to enforceable policy logic. The Product Director wants a compelling conference narrative. Procurement has found several rule-engine vendors. Engineering wants to avoid accidental platform sprawl, and Compliance wants strong audit over how policy becomes system behavior. You are expected to keep the conversation on a defensible path.

questions.prompt: The Product Director says: "The story should be simple: upload a policy PDF and the system enforces it automatically." How do you respond?

options.text: That is a compelling message, so we should design for straight-through automation from the document. If the system can parse the policy and apply it without human involvement, the product story becomes much stronger and we avoid operational bottlenecks. It also gives Procurement and Product a bigger platform narrative and postpones the ownership and approval questions until the engine is already chosen.
options.explanation: The desire for a clean story is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts a slogan before translating it into the real steps of extraction, review, approval, and enforcement.

options.text: I would translate that slogan into a governed pipeline: extract candidate rules, review them in a structured form, approve them explicitly, and only then enforce them. The magic is not the requirement; trustworthy policy change is. We should not let extraction masquerade as authorisation.
options.explanation: This demonstrates translating a slogan into a requirement and separating authoring from enforcement. The answer keeps the room grounded in audit and ownership rather than in a conference soundbite.

options.text: Automatic extraction is too risky, so I would rule it out entirely and have engineering implement every rule by hand. That keeps the behavior under tight technical control even if policy change stays slower.
options.explanation: The concern about safety is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it protects control by jumping to a blunt implementation choice instead of framing the safer middle ground.

questions.prompt: Procurement says: "If we are doing this at all, let us buy an enterprise rules engine that other domains can use later as well." What do you say?

options.text: Yes, because a shared engine gives us leverage beyond Expenses. Even if the first use case is narrow, selecting the enterprise platform now will save the organisation from building separate rule solutions across teams. It also gives Procurement and Product a bigger platform narrative and postpones the ownership and approval questions until the engine is already chosen.
options.explanation: The reuse instinct is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it turns a bounded Expenses decision into an enterprise platform commitment before the current domain has justified that step.

options.text: I would keep the decision on the Expenses problem first and evaluate the option spectrum from simple configuration through to a specialised rules product. If the volatility, ownership, and audit needs in this domain justify a dedicated engine, we can say that plainly without pretending we have already solved every future policy problem in the company.
options.explanation: This demonstrates presenting the option spectrum and anchoring on criteria. The answer forces the heavier shared-platform proposal to earn its place instead of arriving under the banner of reuse.

options.text: Let us postpone the architecture call until we have surveyed how every other policy-heavy team might use the product. Once that enterprise landscape is clear, we can decide whether the rules engine belongs in Expenses or somewhere broader.
options.explanation: The desire for strategic alignment is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already frame the current decision without waiting for a whole-enterprise catalogue of hypothetical users.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Manager says: "To move faster, we should code the rules directly into the service for phase one and think about configuration later." How do you respond?

options.text: That is probably the safest delivery route. Hard-coded logic keeps the implementation simple now, and if the rules change more often than expected we can externalise them after launch. It also gives Procurement and Product a bigger platform narrative and postpones the ownership and approval questions until the engine is already chosen.
options.explanation: The delivery instinct is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts an implementation shortcut before testing how volatile the rules are, who owns them, and how much audit the change process requires.

options.text: I would test that choice against volatility, ownership, and audit. If policy analysts need to review and adjust the rules regularly, hard-coding them makes the service the bottleneck. If the rules are genuinely stable and engineering-owned, code may be fine. We should let the criteria decide rather than defaulting to the fastest-looking implementation.
options.explanation: This demonstrates applying the 5 decision criteria directly in the room. The answer neither romanticises configuration nor defaults to code; it uses ownership and volatility to choose the appropriate weight of solution.

options.text: We should probably do a few more workshops with policy authors before answering that. Until we have a fuller set of examples, it is premature to say whether code or configuration is the better long-term fit.
options.explanation: The wish for evidence is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already frame the decision with criteria and ask the workshops to validate, not replace, that framing.

questions.prompt: Compliance asks: "If extraction produces a high-confidence structured rule, can it go live automatically so policy changes move faster?" What is your response?

options.text: Yes, if the confidence score is high enough we should trust the automation. That gives the business the fastest change path and proves the extraction investment is delivering meaningful operational value.
options.explanation: The speed goal is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it treats extraction confidence as a substitute for accountable approval and weakens the audit chain for policy change.

options.text: I would keep approval explicit even if extraction confidence is strong. Confidence can prioritise review, but it should not replace business sign-off on what rule is being enforced. The policy change path needs accountable approval, not only a machine score.
options.explanation: This demonstrates protecting ownership and audit boundaries. The answer allows automation to assist the flow without letting extraction become the authoriser of live business policy.

options.text: That question is sensitive enough that we should escalate it to governance and avoid taking a position in this meeting. Once the wider policy board publishes guidance, we can align the workflow to that decision. It also gives the governance board a cleaner escalation story, which is part of why that deferral can sound prudent in the room.
options.explanation: The caution is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already state the default principle that approval remains explicit and then let governance confirm the same boundary.

questions.prompt: The room asks you to close. Which summary is strongest?

options.text: We are treating document upload as the start of a governed extraction-and-review pipeline, not as automatic enforcement; we will choose any rules technology against the Expenses criteria before widening the scope to an enterprise platform; we will test code versus configuration using volatility, ownership, and audit; and live policy changes will still require explicit approval. That gives the team a clear architecture direction.
options.explanation: This demonstrates effective meeting closure. The answer summarises the choices, the criteria, and the safe default boundary so the team can proceed without reheating the same slogans.

options.text: There are still enough open issues that the better course is to pause and return with a broader vendor study, more policy examples, and a governance view on automation before we commit to any direction.
options.explanation: The caution is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect already has enough to define the governing principles even if vendor selection details remain open.

options.text: To avoid future redesign, we should choose the broadest path now: enterprise rules engine, automatic activation for high-confidence extraction, and a general policy platform story that other domains can join later. That will make the investment easier to justify.
options.explanation: The desire for strategic justification is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it combines several heavier decisions into one package and lets future-proofing outrun the actual requirement.
